content:
Let $x[n]$ be a discrete-time signal whose $z$-transform is $X(z)$.
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

options.content: The discrete-time Fourier transform (DTFT) of $x[n]$ always exists
The region of convergence (RoC) of $X(z)$ contains neither poles nor zeros
The discrete-time Fourier transform (DTFT) exists if the region of convergence (RoC) contains the unit circle
If $x[n]=\alpha \delta[n]$, where $\delta[n]$ is the unit impulse and $\alpha$ is a scalar, then the region of convergence (RoC) is the entire $z$-plane

explanation:
The discrete-time Fourier transform (DTFT) exists if the region of convergence (RoC) contains the unit circle
If $x[n]=\alpha \delta[n]$, where $\delta[n]$ is the unit impulse and $\alpha$ is a scalar, then the region of convergence (RoC) is the entire $z$-plane

content:
For a causal discrete-time LTI system with transfer function

$H(z) = \frac{2z^2 + 3}{\left(z + \frac{1}{3}\right)\left(z - \frac{1}{3}\right)}$

which of the following statements is/are true?

options.content: The system is a minimum phase system.
The initial value of the impulse response is 2.
The final value of the impulse response is 0.

explanation:
Given : Transfer function of causal discrete LTI system

$$ H(\mathrm{z})=\frac{2 z^2+3}{\left(z+\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(z-\frac{1}{3}\right)} $$

(i) For stability of system Poles of given system are

$$ z=\frac{1}{3}, z=\frac{-1}{3} $$

Hence, $|z|=\frac{1}{3}$

Since all the poles are lying inside the unit circle $|z|=1$. Hence the system is stable

Hence, option (A) is correct.

(ii) Final value theorem is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} & h(\infty)=\lim _{z \rightarrow 1}\left(1-z^{-1}\right) H(z) \\ & h(\infty)=\lim _{z \rightarrow 1}\left(1-z^{-1}\right) \frac{2 z^2+3}{\left(z+\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(z-\frac{1}{3}\right)} \\ & h(\infty)=0 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, option (D) is correct.

(iii) Initial value theorem is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} & h(0)=\lim _{z \rightarrow \infty} H(z) \\ & h(0)=\lim _{z \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2 z^2+3}{\left(z+\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(z-\frac{1}{3}\right)} \\ & h(0)=\lim _{z \rightarrow \infty} \frac{z^2\left[2+\frac{3}{z^2}\right]}{z^2\left(1+\frac{1}{3 z}\right)\left(1-\frac{1}{3 z}\right)} \\ & h(0)=\lim _{z \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\left[2+\frac{3}{z^2}\right]}{\left[1+\frac{1}{3 z}\right]\left[1-\frac{1}{3 z}\right]} \\ & h(0)=\frac{2}{1}=2 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, option (C) is correct.

(iv) For minimum phase system

$$ H(z)=\frac{2 z^2+3}{\left(z+\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(z-\frac{1}{3}\right)} $$

Poles of given system are :

$$ z=\frac{1}{3}, z=-\frac{1}{3} $$

$$ |z|=\frac{1}{3} $$

Zeros of given system are :

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 z^2+3=0 \\ & 2 z^2=-3 \\ & z^2=\frac{-3}{2} \\ & z= \pm i \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} \\ & |z|=1.22 \end{aligned} $$

All the poles are inside the unit circle $|z|=1$ but zeros are lying outside the unit circle. Hence system is not a minimum phase system.

Hence option (B) is not correct. Hence, the correct options are (A), (C) and (D).

content:
Which one of the following pole-zero plots corresponds to the transfer function of an LTI system characterized by the input-output difference equation given below?

$$ y[n]=\sum_{k=0}^3(-1)^k x[n-k] $$
explanation:
Method 1

Given : $y[n]=\sum_{k=0}^3(-1)^k \delta[n-k]$

To find impulse response, substitution $x[n]=\delta[n]$ in the given input output relation.

$$ \begin{aligned} & h[n]=\sum_{k=0}^3(-1)^k \delta[n-k] \\ & h[n]=\delta[n]-\delta[n-1]+\delta[n-2]-\delta[n-3] \end{aligned} $$

Taking $z$-transform both sides to find the transfer function of the system,

$$ \begin{aligned} & H(z)=1-z^{-1}+z^{-2}-z^{-3} \\ & H(z)=\frac{z^3-z^2+z-1}{z^3} \\ & \left.H(z)\right|_{z=+1}=\frac{1-1+1-1}{1}=0 \end{aligned} $$

So, the system function has a zero at $z=+1$ that indicates that the low frequency gain of filter in zero.

So it will be a high pass filter. To check, finding $H(z)$ at $\omega=\pi$ or $z=-1$

$$ \left.H(z)\right|_{z=-1}=\frac{-1-1-1-1}{-1}=4 $$

So the system behaves as a HPF and it will not have zero at $z=-1$.

The only option satisfying this is option (B).

Hence, the correct option is (B).

Method 2

Given : $y[n]=\sum_{k=0}^3(-1)^k x(n-k)$

$$ y(n)=x(n)-x(n-1)+x(n-2)-x(n-3) $$

Taking $z$-transform on both sides,

$$ \begin{aligned} & Y(z)=\left(1-z^{-1}+z^{-2}-z^{-3}\right) X(z) \\ & \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)}=1-z^{-1}+z^{-2}-z^{-3} \\ & H(z)=1-z^{-1}+z^{-2}-z^{-3}=\frac{z^3-z^2+z-1}{z^3} \end{aligned} $$

$H(z)$ has a pole of order 3 at $z=0$.

To find zero's, equate numerator to zero.

$$ \begin{aligned} & z^3-z^2+z-1=0 \\ & (z-1)\left(z^2+1\right)=0 \\ & z=1 \text { and } z= \pm j \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad H(z)$ has zero's at $z=1$ and $z= \pm j$ Hence, the correct option is (B).

options.content:

and
4$$\angle 0^\circ $$.
It is stable only when the impulse response is two-sided.
It has constant phase response over all frequencies.

It has constant phase response over the entire
z-plane.

The transfer function of a stable discrete - time LTI system is $H(z)=\frac{K(z-\alpha)}{(z+0.5)}$ where $K$ and $\alpha$ are real numbers. The value of $\alpha$ (rounded off to one decimal place) with $|\alpha|>1$, for which magnitude response of the system is constant over all frequencies, is $\_\_\_\_$ .
explanation:
Given transfer function for discrete time LTI system

$$ H(z)=\frac{K(z-\alpha)}{(z+0.5)} $$

Where ' $K$ ' and ' $\alpha$ ' are real numbers.

As it is asked to find value of ' $\alpha$ ' such that the magnitude of response is constant over all frequencies, so we have to find value of ' $\alpha$ ' for which the given LTI system will behave as an All Pass Filter.

Location of poles and zeros follows the reciprocal conjugate relationship as poles and zeros are symmetrical about the unit circle.

$$ p=\frac{1}{z^*} \quad \text { where }\left\{\begin{array}{l} P=\text { location of pole } \\ Z=\text { location of zero } \end{array}\right. $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { For given system }\\ &p=-0.5 \quad z=\alpha \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad-0.5=\frac{1}{\alpha^*}\\ &\text { Given, ' } \alpha \text { ' is real, so, } \alpha^*=\alpha\\ &-0.5=\frac{1}{\alpha} \quad \therefore \alpha=-2 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Hence, the value of } \alpha \text { is } \mathbf{- 2} \text {. } $$
